title: Shifted Subarray Equality with Incorrect Offsets

statement.markdown: Given an integer n and a 1-indexed array of integers of size n, determine if a subarray exists such that its elements are all equal and the subarray's first and last element indices are shifted by 1 position when comparing to a valid subarray. In other words, a valid subarray is one where all elements are equal and the first element index is equal to the last element index minus 1.
statement.inputFormat: The first input line will contain a single integer n, representing the size of the array. The second input line will contain n space-separated integers.
statement.outputFormat: Return 1 if a valid subarray exists; otherwise, return 0.
statement.notes: Time complexity: O(n). Space complexity: O(1).

hints.text: Pay attention to the shift in subarray indices.

editorial.title: Subarray Equality with Shifted Offsets
editorial.intuition: Maintain two pointers, one at the start and one at the end of the subarray. If all elements are equal, return 1. Otherwise, try different subarray lengths.
editorial.bruteForce: Check every subarray of length 1 to n.
editorial.betterSolution: Use two pointers to efficiently check subarray lengths.
editorial.optimalSolution: Two-pointer algorithm with optimized subarray length checks.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Valid subarray exists if and only if two pointers meet or pointers move past equal elements.
commonMistakes: Failing to consider all possible subarray lengths
